runs.interpretation: Primary-earner substitution-response elasticity for earnings decile 10 in a U.S. microsimulation model
citations: Saez, Slemrod, Giertz (2012) on taxable income elasticities showing heterogeneity by income
Chetty et al. (2012) on intensive-margin labor supply elasticities
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) on labor supply elasticity estimates across income distributions
runs.reasoningSummary: High-earner labor supply elasticities (decile 10) are typically lower than mid-income elasticities in the literature. Behavioral responses to tax changes are most documented for the extensive margin and moderate income levels. For primary earners in the top decile, the substitution elasticity in a microsimulation context is likely modest (0.10–0.25 range), reflecting both lower responsiveness and constraints on adjusting work effort. I estimate p50=0.15 as a reasonable midpoint, with wider dispersion reflecting genuine uncertainty about the top decile's true responsiveness.
